It's really good, really long, very in-depth, but it basically packages a lot of the things that we do on the model performance side and on the infrastructure side to make things so fast and so reliable, so the way that I like to think of these two is the model runtime, to me, is really, like, synonymous with the work of our model performance team and a lot of the model level optimizations that we do to make things super fast.

And then on the infrastructure side, where that comes in, it does also play a role for performance, is the consistency of that performance.
So let's say that you want to have like 400 millisecond latency on an AI phone call.
Well, you want to have that latency, whether you have five users or 5,000 or 5 million.
Like, let's say you go viral.
You want to be able to meet that demand and keep meeting that latency SLA.
And in order to do that, you need to be able to scale efficiently.
You need to be able to have access to the compute.
That compute needs to be located close to your customers.
You need to have the networking in place, the request routing in place, the cold starts in place.
And all of that is an infrastructure challenge.
That's not really at the model side.
That's on the infrastructure side.
So the infrastructure does touch the performance from that perspective of the latency, of the consistency of the latency.

you run out of capacity, you need to be able to scale elsewhere.

Outside of latency and throughput, what are other kind of performance-related metrics you guys track for clients?
I'd say there's really, those are sort of the two high-level categories.
Throughput, you can think of, throughput to me is mainly just throughput.
There's different ways to measure throughput.
can think of, like, overall throughput.
You can think of token throughput.
But I think generally we just do, like, yeah.
I mean, it depends on the context.
If it's overall, like, request throughput or token-level throughput.
So we just use throughput sort of generically.
But latency, you can break down into, like, end-to-end latency.
So that's the time from, like, when you send a request.
And
And you get the full response back.
And then you're going to have another really common measure of latency is going to be time to first token, for instance, token for LLM.
Or if you're doing a voice AI application, it's going to be time to first byte.
So the first time that a byte of audio is turned back to you.

And those are really important for the LLM and the voice AI use cases because as soon as you start hearing a response or seeing a response, the end user still has that feeling of like they're getting a response.
And those are typically abbreviated as TTFT or TTFB.
And what about cold starts, Rachel?
Like it's also like performance related, right?
It is.
We do talk about cold starts.
We don't put numbers on them.

We're going to do more optimizations around cold starts.
And it's just a very high variability.
So, you know, some cold start, especially it depends on the model, depends on the workload.
depends on if the person's doing caching or not.

You know, if we can cache the weight somehow, if we, you know, have enough, if they have enough load, then we might have like a warm replica somewhere, meaning that it's already kind of prepared for them.
more on the model.

So it could be anywhere on the scale of like 20 seconds to three minutes, and you know, how impressive that is also depends on the model, and you know, so we've been a little bit wary of how we talk about cold starts, but yeah, Marylise, you're right, that's definitely something we should talk about in performance, just, you know, when we put a number on it, we need to be careful.

And then you can hand that transcription off to the actual model that's going to be doing the speech-to-text transcription.
And then you can send that back or you can like iterate over it.

You can use an LLM afterwards to do like quality control on the end product, and you can do all of that like on different hardware and different auto-scaling, and that just totally speeds up the process and makes it more cost-efficient because now you have, you know, the right type of hardware running for each step in your workflow.

We can do sort of an in-between that's called single tenant, meaning that the cluster is just for you.

A lot of it with the self-hosted is going to be, you know, IP, regulated industries, healthcare, finance.
If you have anything that just can't leave your VPC, maybe you have, like, you know, something in place with clients already.
That they're fine being in the AWS ecosystem, but they can't leave that.
So, you you kind of just want to stick on what you already have, what they have their customers that have already signed agreements for, that they're willing to be in that environment.
So they want to stay in that environment.
That's typically in the self-hosted case.
On the, you know, on the hybrid case is that if you run out of capacity and you do have some workloads that aren't as sensitive, then you could spill over and run that capacity.
And our cloud, as I don't have, you know, if you're using like a really high-end GPU, those are typically harder to get, especially on demand.
So if you have a launch or something like that, then you have sort of that flex capacity.
The single tenet is sort of a compromise there.
So you don't have such strict security or IP requirements that it actually needs to be in your VPC, but you still want to have that assurance of like, you it's just a cluster running for you.
There's no other customer or any other workload that's going to be on that cluster.
And then BaseTen Cloud, I mean, of course, still very, very secure.
It's typically a technicality when people need to do the self-hosted.

But, like, if they do BaseTen Cloud, it's going to be us choosing where the loads go.

So that's really where you get the whole benefits of BaseTen.

So I know we already went a little bit into our different products, but just to give a little bit more, shed a little bit more light on the use cases here, so we typically position model APIs around people who are just sort of testing something out, maybe there's a new model, you want to see how it performs in your workflow, see how the quality is, and maybe you're still building a POC, you're just very early in your journey and you don't have the amount of scale that would justify having like an actual dedicated deployment running for you, that's when we position our model APIs around.
For the dedicated deployment side, that's when you actually have that scale, you know, can scale pretty much limitlessly.

So if you have like, you know, you're launching something, you already have a big, you know, consumer base, you're a scale up in enterprise, then the actual dedicated deployment where you have that hardware running for you and you can control the auto scaling settings and everything around it, that's when that would come in.

And then training, because our training product is a little bit lower level and it's a little bit closer to the actual training infrastructure, we position it for engineers, like it's not, you know, one of the...
These very abstracted UIs, it's not for, you know, like, tinkerers or people who want, like, the lowest lift to try to sort of fine-tune a little model.

It's really, you know, for people who are a little bit more serious and they, you know, they're comfortable writing the code, they're comfortable working at a lower level, and that's when we position our, or where we position our training product.

The three things that we've been also sort of hitting on whenever we hit on any of our products or any of our offerings is these three things.
Performance.
Everything is super fast.
Best in class performance.
The lowest latency.
The highest throughput.

So this is really going to be the number one thing that we want to emphasize.
And then from their reliability, we have better reliability than, I think, all of our competitors.
The way we power that is through something that
We call multi-cloud capacity management, which essentially abstracts all of the different clouds that we run on so we can just scale models up on any cloud.
We basically treat them as sort of one global cloud as opposed to the nine different ones that lets us scale beyond capacity constraints, that lets us scale beyond hardware failures.
You know, when a cloud goes down, our competitors go down for hours and we just scale models up in a different cloud.
So we power really good reliability.
We typically say that we power four nines reliability.
And then, of course, cost efficiency.
This one, to me, goes really hand in hand with the performance.
What I like to stress is that even if you're going to spend a little bit more on hardware, let's say like you're using a B200 and some people might say, oh, well, it's just a more expensive GPU.

You know, but if you're just like, let's say you're 1.5 X-ing the cost, but you get three times the throughput, you're actually going to be saving costs overall because things are just so efficient and you're using the hardware maximally.
And a lot of our tooling, if not all of our tooling, is built around that.

So the thing here is that, like the great thing here is really just it lets you chain models together and while also keeping them very modular.
So you can chain these different models together as individual, we call them chainlets.
A chainlet is one model or processing step.
It's run on its own independent hardware.
It scales independently.
So let's say you have something that's like very CPU bound.
You can deploy that on a CPU rather than having that block a very expensive, you know, by comparison, expensive GPU in the meantime.

So you can separate those different parts of the workload, really put them on the hardware that's best suited for them, scale them as they need to be scaled, and then, you know, link them all together so that they're calling each other directly.
So you don't have.
That network overhead of like actually sending data in and out of the cloud, right?
They're actually just remote services that are calling each other directly.
And again, these are going to be for these use cases like, you know, AI phone calling or like any of those agent use cases or rags.
